summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes developers who publish sample code with empty, default AssemblyInfo metadata. He argues that if you're taking the time to make code publicly available, you should spend the extra 60 seconds to fill in basic assembly information like title, description, and company. The post also notes a gap in .NET's default assembly attributes: the absence of URL and email fields, which would require custom attributes to include.
key_points: Empty AssemblyInfo files in published code signal laziness and provide no useful metadata
Filling out assembly metadata takes minimal effort (60 seconds) relative to the value it provides
The .NET framework's default assembly attributes are incomplete, lacking URL and email fields
Proper versioning and metadata are basic professional courtesies when sharing code
money_quotes: So, basically, you're compiling into an assembly DLL or EXE with nothing but crappy, default metadata and versioning.
If you're going to the effort of making your code publicly available, take an additional 60 seconds to fill out the AssemblyInfo.
My only regret with assembly attributes is that they really should have included URL and email attributes by default.
key_insight: If you care enough to share your code publicly, care enough to fill in the basic metadata that helps others understand what they're using.
